summary: Scorching leads and monster Not Fade Away in a tight May ’77 set
blurb: The band delivers scorching lead work on Sugaree, Jack‑A‑Roe and Bertha while the second set erupts with a monster Not Fade Away and seamless Terrapin Station > Estimated Prophet > Eyes Of The World flow. Jerry’s tone is flawless, Phil anchors the groove, and Keith’s keyboards add depth throughout.
review: May 26, 1977 at the Baltimore Civic Center captured one of the spring‑'77 era’s most consistently powerful performances. From the opening "The Music Never Stopped" through a first‑set packed with tight renditions of "Sugaree," "Jack‑A‑Roe," and a fiery "Bertha," the Dead were locked in, with Garcia’s lead guitar described as “scorching,” “orgasmic,” and “perfectly toned.” The second set turned up the heat: a rousing "Samson & Delilah" gave way to a soulful "High Time," then the band launched into an explosive run of "Terrapin Station > Estimated Prophet > Eyes Of The World" before a thunderous "Not Fade Away" that surged straight into a classic jam. Critics across five soundboard recordings and two audience captures agree the night was highlighted by Jerry’s lead work, Mickey’s “smashing” drumming, and Keith’s deep‑sounding keyboards, all wrapped in crystal‑clear mixes that make this show a must‑listen for any Deadhead.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All seven recordings are flagged with positive sentiment, using language like 'scorching', 'monster', 'stellar', and 'exceptional'. Multiple sources praise the same standout moments (Sugaree/Jack‑A‑Roe/Bertha leads, Not Fade Away jam, Terrapin > Estimated > Eyes sequence). No consistent negative comments about performance quality appear, only isolated song‑specific critiques that do not affect overall show assessment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard with an exceptional mix that captures the band’s tight playing, especially Garcia’s leads on Sugaree, Jack‑A‑Roe and Bertha, plus a vivid second‑set energy.

key_highlights: Garcia’s scorching lead work on Sugaree, Jack‑A‑Roe and Bertha
Monster Not Fade Away that launches into a seamless Eyes Of The World jam
Terrapin Station > Estimated Prophet > Eyes Of The World sequence flows flawlessly
High Time delivers a smoky groove that anchors the second set

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone is described as perfect and his leads on Sugaree, Bertha and Jack‑A‑Roe are called ‘scorching’ and ‘orgasmic,’ stealing the show with inspired noodling throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Phil provides a solid, sometimes louder‑than‑mix bass foundation; reviewers note his opening cannon‑shot on the low end and dynamic interplay with Jerry, especially during Eyes Of The World.
band_performance.Bob: Weir is praised for strong vocals and guitar work, with audible interplay with Jerry that adds depth to jams like Not Fade Away and the Terrapin sequence.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboard work sits well in the mix, adding depth on Sugaree and delivering the era‑defining organ textures that enhance the second‑set flow.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey’s drumming is called ‘smashing,’ driving the energy of Not Fade Away and providing tight support throughout the jam sections.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
